title: Data Types
summary: Choose representations that match the stored values.
refresher: Common C++ types include `int`, `double`, `char`, `bool`, and `std::string`. Conversions can be implicit, but `static_cast` makes an intentional conversion visible.

blocks.body: Common C++ types include `int`, `double`, `char`, `bool`, and `std::string`. Conversions can be implicit, but `static_cast` makes an intentional conversion visible. Choose representations that match the stored values.

Types describe both meaning and representation. `int` is for whole numbers, `double` keeps fractional values, `char` stores one character, and `bool` stores a truth value. Converting to a type with a smaller range or less precision can change the value, which is called narrowing.

blocks.caption: Casting one operand before division preserves the fractional result.
Keep fractional data in a matching type.

blocks.body: The wrong declaration converts the decimal to an integer and stores three. The corrected declaration preserves the fraction. Numeric limits also matter: an integer cannot represent every possible whole number, so select a type whose range fits the data and avoid relying on overflow.

blocks.title: C++ detail to check
blocks.body: Storing a decimal in an int discards its fractional part. The compiler may allow that conversion even when it is not what the calculation needs.

blocks.why: The char type represents one character value such as a letter.
Conversion to int truncates the fractional part rather than rounding it.
